diagnostic.density_assessment: Slightly low (−1.7 % at 298 K, −2.1 % at 323 K) but follows the correct temperature trend.
diagnostic.hvap_assessment: Significantly under‑predicted (−7.8 % at 298 K, −12.5 % at 323 K); this is the most problematic property.
diagnostic.surface_tension_assessment: Excellent agreement (≤0.7 % deviation).
warnings: Heat of vaporization deviation is large and dominates the composite error.
Dipole moment is modestly low, indicating insufficient charge separation.
High variance across forks for hvap and density suggests strong sensitivity to ε and bond‑stiffness parameters.
Some forks produced 10000‑value outputs, indicating crashes at extreme parameter limits.
recommendations: Make the Lennard‑Jones epsilon more attractive (more negative) for both OCB and MS2_OCB by ~5–10 % (e.g., change from –0.65 to –0.70 to –0.75).
Increase the dummy‑bead bond lengths (MS2_DUP_bl and OCB_DUN_bl) from 1.40 Å to 1.6–1.8 Å to raise the dipole moment.
If dipole still low after length increase, raise dummy charges modestly (e.g., from ±0.14 e to ±0.16 e).
Increase the bond‑stiffness constants (MS2_DUP_kb, OCB_DUN_kb) proportionally (e.g., from 140 kcal mol‑1 Å‑2 to 150–160) to keep the longer dummy bonds stable.
Keep OCB_rminby2 and MS2_OCB_rmin near their current values; they are already in a reasonable size range.
Run a short validation after each adjustment to ensure no new instability appears.
Consider expanding the search bounds for ε (more negative) and dummy bond lengths to allow the optimizer to explore the suggested region.

proposal.reasoning: We are still in the exploration phase and the diagnostics highlight three deficiencies: (i) under‑predicted heat of vaporization (needs stronger LJ attraction), (ii) slightly low density (also helped by stronger attraction), and (iii) modest dipole moment (needs larger charge separation and longer dummy‑core bonds). To address these, I varied the LJ epsilon for both OCB and MS2‑OCB, increased the dummy‑core bond lengths to the allowed maximum, raised the opposite dummy charges, and stiffened the dummy‑core bonds to keep the longer bonds stable. I also explored the effect of a higher MS2‑OCB bond stiffness and modest changes to angle force constants and equilibrium angles to tune packing. One fork stays close to the current best (exploitation) while the other seven explore different combinations of the recommended changes, including a control fork with a less attractive epsilon to retain diversity. All values respect the prescribed bounds and avoid known crash regions.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Making the Lennard‑Jones epsilon more negative strengthens intermolecular cohesion, which should raise density, heat of vaporization and surface tension. Lengthening the dummy‑core bonds (r₀) and increasing the magnitude of opposite dummy charges enlarges the molecular dipole moment, improving the dipole‑related target. Raising the dummy‑core bond force constants keeps the longer bonds from excessive stretch, preserving stability. Adjusting angle force constants and equilibrium angles fine‑tunes molecular packing, helping to balance the surface‑tension response while the stronger epsilon tends to increase it.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Stronger ε will increase ΔHvap and density toward the experimental values. Larger dummy‑core bond lengths together with higher opposite charges will raise the dipole moment to the target ~3.96 D. Higher bond stiffness and modest angle adjustments will maintain stability and prevent crashes while allowing the structural changes to manifest.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by running MD simulations for the eight parallel forks listed below and evaluating density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment at 298 K and 323 K.
